results.seo_title: How COBALT Democratizes Robot Learning via Cloud-Based Teleoperation…
results.meta_description: COBALT enables scalable robot learning at scale using smartphones, reducing teleoperation cost and improving data quality.
results.body_html:
COBALT enables scalable robot learning at scale using smartphones, reducing teleoperation cost and improving data quality.

Concurrent teleoperation by multiple users on a single GPU Supports up to 8 concurrent users per GPU with sub-100 ms end-to-end latency Operators can connect from nearly anywhere on Earth using commonly available devices

Improved Data Quality COBALT logs a suite of real-time metrics to automatically filter suboptimal demonstrations.

Faster Data Collection Phone-based teleoperation performs comparably to or better than specialized hardware, enabling faster data collection.

Scalability and Performance COBALT supports 256 simulated clients across 8 GPUs, underscoring the system's ability to scale across hardware and within individual servers.

Phone-based teleoperation performs comparably to or better than specialized hardware in terms of data collection quality. The structured user training curriculum significantly improves data collection quality.

source.title: COBALT: Crowdsourcing Robot Learning via Cloud-Based Teleoperation with Smartphones
source.summary: Researchers have developed COBALT, a platform that enables crowdsourced robot learning via cloud-based teleoperation using smartphones. The system allows multiple users to control robots in real-time and collect high-quality demonstration data, which can be used to train imitation learning algorithms. By leveraging vectorized environments and scalable infrastructure, COBALT reduces the cost of teleoperation and can support dozens of concurrent users with low latency. A large-scale pilot dataset was also collected using smartphones across nine countries, demonstrating the platform's potential for democratizing robot learning at scale.

The company develops and implements AI-powered tools to detect and prevent such manipulation. Collaboration with regulatory bodies ensures the safety of financial transactions.

Protecting Health Outcomes with Explainable AI

Explainability in Medical Diagnosis AI models can provide transparent explanations for medical diagnoses, reducing the risk of misdiagnosis.

Data Protection and Privacy DeepMind's AI solutions prioritize data protection and privacy to safeguard sensitive health information.

Addressing Cybersecurity Threats with Quantum Computing Quantum computing can help accelerate the development of more secure AI systems, reducing the risk of cyber threats. The integration of quantum computing and AI can enhance the security of financial transactions and health data.

Safeguarding Smart Healthcare Systems

DeepMind's research emphasizes the importance of securing IoT communications in smart healthcare systems. The company develops innovative solutions to address vulnerabilities in BioMEMS, ensuring the integrity of medical diagnostics and treatment. Collaboration with experts in healthcare and technology ensures the development of safe and effective AI-powered tools.

results.seo_title: Adobe's Conversational AI Agent: A Mediocre Design Intern
results.meta_description: The Verge reviews Adobe's conversational AI assistant, finding it surprisingly interactive and forthcoming about its limitations.
results.body_html:
The Verge reviews Adobe's conversational AI assistant, finding it surprisingly interactive and forthcoming about its limitations.

Interactive Conversations

  • The Firefly AI Assistant engages users in conversations, describing the scene and explaining the editing process step by step.
  • The chatbot mentions specific tools from Photoshop and Lightroom using established editing terminology.
  • The assistant is surprisingly forthcoming about its limitations, offering alternative courses of action for tasks it cannot perform.

Limitations and Transparency

  • The Firefly AI Assistant admits when it can't perform a task, such as separating objects from an image into separate layers.
  • The chatbot explains the pros and cons of different approaches and redirects itself to use alternative methods.
  • The assistant provides detailed explanations of its editing process, including the fact that certain techniques aren't working as expected.

Technical Details

"I notice the gaussian blur approach isn’t giving me true transparent cutouts — it’s outputting full-image PNGs,"
is an example of the Firefly AI Assistant's transparency and willingness to explain its limitations.

Future Potential

  • The conversational AI assistant has the potential to improve user engagement and experience in design applications.
  • The technology could be used to deliver automated human support factors in clinical psychology interventions.
  • The Firefly AI Assistant may pave the way for more advanced AI-powered design tools in the future.

Read time: 7 minutes

source.title: Adobe’s conversational AI agent is a mediocre design intern
source.summary: AI image tools are often designed for non-designers, leaving designers feeling left out of the creative process. Impact on Internet Traffic

Characteristics Description
Machine-generated internet traffic Dominating the future of the internet, with human users taking a backseat.
Cloud infrastructure redesign AWS and Cloudflare are leading the charge in this effort.

Emerging Trends in Autonomous Vehicles

This shift towards autonomous vehicles will have significant implications for industries such as transportation and logistics.

The lack of security measures in IoT devices poses a significant risk, making users skeptical and vulnerable to security attacks.

results.seo_title: Guiding Planning with Dynamically Changing Norms in Human-AI Settings
results.meta_description: Researchers present an approach to guiding planning with dynamically changing norms in human-AI settings, contributing a defeasible calculus for resolving…
results.body_html:
AI agents must know and consider human norms during planning to safely interact with humans.

Defeasible Calculus for Resolving Normative Conflicts

A defeasible calculus is proposed to resolve normative conflicts, providing a framework for reasoning about norms in human-AI settings. The calculus is based on erotetic inferences, which are similar to the way norms of epistemic rationality are modeled. The approach aims to provide a more comprehensive understanding of norm-guided planning and its application in human-AI interactions.

Guard Rails for Plans Using Dynamically Changing Norms

Normative Conflict Solution using dynamically changing norms

Dynamically changing norm A conflicts with norm B The calculus provides a solution by considering the defeasible inference from A to B.

Norm C is updated based on new evidence The guard rail approach ensures that plans are adjusted accordingly, taking into account the updated norm.

Empirical Evaluation with SocialBot Our approach is evaluated empirically using the AI agent SocialBot on a natural language dialogue task, demonstrating its effectiveness in guiding planning with dynamically changing norms.

source.title: Reasoning and Planning with Dynamically Changing Norms
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new approach to help AI agents plan safely in human-AI interactions by considering dynamic norms that can change over time. The approach uses a defeasible calculus to resolve normative conflicts and incorporates these changing norms as guardrails for plans. This method aims to improve the safety and effectiveness of AI systems in interacting with humans, particularly in natural language dialogue tasks.

results.seo_title: How Managed Autonomy Can Ensure Reliable AI Systems
results.meta_description: A new theory of managed autonomy explores the architectural vulnerability of unbounded autonomy in agentic AI systems, introducing a framework for formalizing…
results.body_html:
Direct answer to the question of managed autonomy in agentic AI systems: The theory of managed autonomy defines intelligent behavior through the formal capacity to detect epistemic drift, suspend reasoning, attempt recovery, and ultimately surrender control when reliability diminishes.

Key Components of Managed Autonomy

  • The SMARt model features four-layer framework: Stable, Meta-cognitive, Assisted, and Regulated states.
  • The model incorporates domain-specific trigger sets to preserve safety in varied operational settings.
  • The timed, guarded Petri net formulation establishes theoretically bounded properties for the system.

Benefits of Managed Autonomy

BenefitDescription
Formalized failure management The SMARt model provides a framework for detecting and responding to failures in agentic AI systems.
Governance reachability The model ensures that the system can be governed and controlled under specified conditions.
Preservation of safety The domain-specific trigger sets ensure that the system operates safely in various operational settings.

Limitations and Challenges

While managed autonomy offers several benefits, it also introduces new challenges and limitations, including the need for adaptive triggers and the potential for escalation.

Read time: 10 minutes

source.title: Intelligence as Managed Autonomy: Failure, Escalation, and Governance for Agentic AI Systems
source.summary: Researchers have identified a challenge in managing autonomous AI systems, particularly in detecting when they are operating outside their reliability bounds. A new theory of "managed autonomy" proposes that intelligent behavior can be defined by an agent's ability to detect uncertainty, suspend reasoning, and surrender control when necessary. The SMARt model, a four-layer framework, has been developed to formalize this concept and demonstrate how architecture can ensure safety and governance in AI systems. By incorporating domain-specific triggers, the SMARt model enables safe expansion of an agent's operational scope over time.

results.seo_title: AssetOpsBench: Evaluating AI Agent Performance in Industrial Reality
results.meta_description: AssetOpsBench bridges the gap between AI agent benchmarks and industrial reality, evaluating agent performance across six critical dimensions of industrial…
results.body_html:
AssetOpsBench is a framework specifically designed to evaluate agent performance across six critical dimensions of industrial applications.

Building Blocks of Industrial Operations

  • Work order summarization and prioritization
  • Task type, output format, category, and sub-agents
  • Multi-agent coordination, failure modes, and data streams

Evaluation Framework and Overall Feedback

AssetOpsBench evaluates agentic systems across six qualitative dimensions designed to reflect real operational constraints in industrial asset management.

Criteria Description
Decision trace quality Evaluates the agent's ability to provide a clear and interpretable decision-making process.
Evidence grounding Assesses the agent's ability to ground its decisions in evidence and data.
Failure awareness Evaluates the agent's ability to recognize and respond to failure modes.
Actionability under incomplete and noisy data Assesses the agent's ability to take action in the presence of incomplete or noisy data.

Failure Modes in Industrial Agentic Workflows

AssetOpsBench explicitly treats failure modes as first-class evaluation signals in agentic industrial workflows.

Failure analysis in AssetOpsBench is implemented through a dedicated trajectory-level pipeline (TrajFM), which combines LLM-based reasoning with statistical clustering to surface interpretable failure patterns from agent execution traces.

Distribution of Failures

Across 881 agent execution traces, failure distribution was as follows:

  • 23.8% agents claimed to have completed tasks despite unsuccessful failure recovery
  • 31.2% agents output success even after unsuccessful failure recovery
  • 94% top-performing agents had 100% tool accuracy
  • 47% multi-agent task accuracy was lower than single-agent task accuracy

Key Error Findings

Agents with access to failure mode databases and maintenance manuals performed better, but RAG knowledge wasn’t always used correctly.

source.title: AssetOpsBench: Bridging the Gap Between AI Agent Benchmarks and Industrial Reality
source.summary: AssetOpsBench: Bridging the Gap Between AI Agent Benchmarks and Industrial Reality

results.seo_title: Unlocking Agentic RL Training for GPT-OSS: A Practical Retrospective
results.meta_description: GPT-OSS agentic RL training challenges and solutions, including PPO on-policy integrity restoration and attention sink support.

The GPT-OSS model has shown comparable performance to OpenAI o3-mini and o4-mini, but its suitability for agentic reinforcement learning training has not yet been validated.

Challenges of GPT-OSS RL Training

  • Restoring PPO on-policy integrity: A fix for MoE log-probability mismatch
  • Correcting training-inference mismatch
  • Attention sink support in FlashAttentionV3
  • Standard attention with sinks (GPT-OSS)

Restoring PPO On-Policy Integrity: A Fix for MoE Log-Probability Mismatch

The Dual Forward Pass and MoE Architecture led to a mismatch between the two log-probabilities, causing non-zero clipping values.

This difference in routing leads to log⁡(π(a∣s))≠log⁡(πold(a∣s)), resulting in a deviated importance ratio.

Correcting Training–Inference Mismatch

Simplifying training to GSM8K, a single-step task without agentic tool use, persisted instability.

This indicates a fundamental issue in the bas

results.seo_title: EgoBench Benchmark Sets New Performance Ceiling for Tool-Using…
results.meta_description: EgoBench benchmark reveals severe performance ceiling for tool-using agents, highlighting capability bottlenecks and limitations in current AI models.
results.body_html:
The EgoBench benchmark sets a new performance ceiling for tool-using agents in AI, with the best model achieving only 30.62% accuracy in the best-performing scenario.

Four Daily Scenarios

  • Task 1: Kitchen - 245 tasks covering various kitchen activities
  • Task 2: Living Room - 245 tasks covering various living room activities
  • Task 3: Bedroom - 245 tasks covering various bedroom activities
  • Task 4: Office - 215 tasks covering various office activities

Three-Stage Synergistic Pipeline

Stage Description
1 Visual perception and tool invocation with multi-hop reasoning
2 Dynamic interaction with users and evaluation of dynamic interaction capabilities
3 Joint application of visual perception and tool-augmented multi-hop reasoning

Deterministic Joint Validation Framework

Established to guarantee objective assessment through process-based and result-based equivalence.

Benchmarking Results

Benchmarking eight SOTA video-MLLM agents on EgoBench reveals a severe performance ceiling, with the best model achieving only 30.62% accuracy in the best-performing scenario.

source.title: EgoBench: An Interactive Egocentric Multimodal Benchmark for Tool-Using Agents
source.summary: Researchers have introduced EgoBench, an interactive multimodal benchmark designed to evaluate the capabilities of tool-using AI agents in open environments. The benchmark comprises 1,045 tasks covering four daily scenarios and a user-agent-tool interaction environment, allowing for assessment of visual perception, multi-hop reasoning, and dynamic interaction. Eight state-of-the-art video-MLLM agents were tested on EgoBench, achieving only 30.62% accuracy in the best-performing scenario, highlighting performance ceilings that need to be addressed.

results.seo_title: Bringing Robotics AI to Embedded Platforms: Dataset Recording, VLA Fin
results.meta_description:
## Bringing Robotics AI to Embedded Platforms: Dataset Recording, VLA Fine‑Tuning, and On‑Device Optimizations

🎥 Dataset Recording: What Actually Matters 1)…
results.body_html:
In synchronous control pipelines, while the VLA is running inference, the arm is idle awaiting commands leading to oscillatory behavior and delayed corrections. To tackle that, asynchronous Inference can enable smooth and continuous motion by dissociating generation from execution. However, to be effective, the end-to-end inference latency must remain shorter than the action execution duration. This temporal constraint therefore sets an upper limit on the model's throughput.

Bringing VLA models to embedded platforms is not a matter of model compression, but a complex systems engineering problem requiring architectural decomposition, latency-aware scheduling, and hardware-aligned execution. Addressing these challenges is essential to translate recent advances in multimodal foundation models into practical and deployable embedded robotic systems.

This guide presents NXP’s hands‑on best practices for recording reliable robotic datasets, fine‑tuning VLA policies (ACT and SmolVLA), and hightlights the real-time performance that NXP i.MX 95 SoC achieves after optimization.

🎥 Dataset Recording: What Actually Matters

High‑quality, consistent data beats “more but messy” data. This section turns hard‑earned lessons into concrete checklists and schemas.

In our case, we recorded a dataset for the task: "Put the tea bag in the mug."

1) Consistency First

Fixed cameras: Use rigid mounts to avoid pose drift. If during recording or evaluation one or more cameras shift because of the robot's vibrations or the operator resetting the environment, you can observe a severe accuracy loss.

Controlled lighting: Set up your environment where you can have as much control as possible on lighting (Fixed light source(s) and far from sunlight that vary during the day).

Strong contrast: Avoid training with “white on white” unless that’s your deployment domain. Maximize contrast between the arm, the object and the environment.

Fixed calibration: Make sure to have backups of your robot and teleoperator calibrations so you don't have to re-record your previous episodes if the code crashes.

Do not cheat: Do not use information the model will not have access to at inference time. During data recording, it is tempting for the operator to rely on direct visual observation of the scene. However, this introduces information that is absent from the dataset. Dataset collection must be restricted to the same camera inputs that will be available to the policy at runtime.

2) Use a Gripper Camera (Highly Recommended)

Moving from scene‑only views to mixed viewpoints increases the global accuracy, but the more cameras you have the more the latency is impacted. Therefore, you must choose right compromise. In our case that balance was reached with 3 cameras:

We strongly recommend using a gripper-mounted camera. It consistently improves success rates on fine manipulation tasks by providing a close, task-relevant viewpoint. Importantly, it is also the camera that most effectively enforces correct data collection practices, allowing the operator to rely exclusively on the robot’s perception rather than observing the scene directly.

When installing a gripper camera, we recommend securing the cable with Velcro or a strain-relief guide to prevent it from obstructing the field of view or becoming disconnected during motion.

3) Improve Prehension

Simple hardware tweaks like heat‑shrink tubing over gripper claws increase friction, reduce roughness, reduce slippage during episodes, and increase task success rate (fewer “almost success” episodes), improving policy learning stability.

4) Diversity & Splits

Vary episodes distribution: Divide your workspace into starting-position clusters, and record at least 10 episodes per cluster. Add diversity by changing the object position and rotation. e.g. we partitioned the robot arm’s reachable workspace into 11 clusters, each measuring 10 × 10 cm.

Differentiate training & validation sets: Policies can easily overfit on the training set, so make sure that the validation set is unseen by the model. e.g. we removed cluster 6 from the training set.

Record the most movements you can: Small VLA models exhibit limited generalization on unseen motion. Therefore, record episodes that cover the wider ranges of degrees of freedom. e.g. we grasped the tea bag either in horizontal or vertical position.

Anticipate failure: Sometimes the policy will not reach the object the first time and will have to "go back to it". We noticed that having 20% of all episodes that corresponds to the case of going back to the object help the model improve overall success rate. e.g. around 20% of our training set corresponds to recovery episodes.

This mirrors best practices across VLA papers and community guides. Here are 3 examples of data diversity within the same cluster:

Starting positions 1 and 2 correspond to different positions within the same cluster. In contrast, during the recovery episode, the robot does not begin in "starting mode"; but is instead already near the mug and should proceed directly to retrieve the tea bag from that location.

🎛️ Fine‑Tuning VLAs

The range providing the best trade-off between accuracy, generalization, and motion smoothness across both the training and validation sets was found for ACT (100 actions per chunk) within a 100k-160k training steps. For SMolVLA training (50 actions per chunk), the trade‑off appears after many more training steps. We found that continuing training slightly past the point where the model begins to overfit tends to improve overall accuracy.

Rule of thumb: choose final checkpoint by evaluating success on both training and validation set, not by training loss.

source.title: Bringing Robotics AI to Embedded Platforms: Dataset Recording, VLA Fine‑Tuning, and On‑Device Optimizations
source.summary: Bringing Robotics AI to Embedded Platforms: Dataset Recording, VLA Fine‑Tuning, and On‑Device Optimizations

results.seo_title: Human-like in-group bias in instruction-tuned language model agents
results.meta_description: Researchers found that instruction-tuned language model agents exhibit human-like in-group bias when interacting with group labels, leading to structural…
results.body_html:
Human-like in-group bias has been observed in instruction-tuned language model agents when interacting with group labels.

Per-turn in-group versus out-group differentials of 5 to 16 percentage points were statistically significant for all six models (Wilcoxon signed-rank, all Benjamini-Hochberg-corrected p less than 0.001). In-group trust biases accumulated into structural inequality in persistent networks through 500 turns of reciprocation.

Results and Implications Group-content targeting as a robust property of instruction-tuned language models across architectures and training regimes.

The study did not investigate the causes of in-group bias. A larger sample size would provide more conclusive results.

source.title: Human-like in-group bias in instruction-tuned language model agents
source.summary: Researchers conducted a simulation study on autonomous AI agents using instruction-tuned language models, which interacted with each other across multiple turns under different conditions. The results showed that when group labels were visible, the agents exhibited in-group bias, favoring actions towards their own group and discriminating against others. This bias was not detectable through standard audits of action logs, but instead manifested as differences in who received actions, leading to structural inequality in the network over time. The study found this phenomenon to be robust across different model architectures and training regimes.

results.body_html:
The new VLA-AD distillation framework reduces model size by 44 imes while maintaining performance, making it an efficient solution for real-time closed-loop control.

Reduces model size by 44 imes Improves inference speedup by 3.28 imes Enhances robustness and deployability

Performance on LIBERO Benchmark Suites

Generalization to Different Teachers Using the same semantic distillation pipeline, VLA-AD generalizes to a different π_0.5-4B teacher, outperforming the teacher on two suites and remaining within 0.53% on libero_goal.

Analysis of Phase-Level Supervision

Phase-level supervision improves student performance by reducing sensitivity to noisy teacher actions Multi-frame directional cues also contribute to improved robustness

source.title: Offline Semantic Guidance for Efficient Vision-Language-Action Policy Distillation
source.summary: Billion-parameter Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies have recently shown impressive performance in robotic manipulation, yet their size and inference cost remain major obstacles for real-time closed-loop control. We introduce \textbf{VLA-AD}, a distillation framework that uses a Vision-Language Model as an offline semantic supervisor to transfer large VLA teachers into lightweight student policies. Instead of relying only on low-level action imitation, VLA-AD augments teacher-provided 7-DoF action targets with high-level semantic guidance, including task phase anchors and multi-frame operating-direction descriptions. These auxiliary signals are used only during training: at test time, the student policy runs independently, with neither the VLA teacher nor the VLM required. We evaluate VLA-AD on three LIBERO benchmark suites. Using OpenVLA-7B as the teacher, our method produces a 158M-parameter student, yielding a $44\times$ reduction in model size while matching the teacher with only a $0.27\%$ average relative gap. The resulting policy runs at 12.5 Hz on an RTX 4090, achieving a $3.28\times$ inference speedup over OpenVLA-7B. We further show that the same semantic distillation pipeline generalizes to a different $\pi_{0.5}$-4B teacher, where the student outperforms the teacher on two suites and remains within $0.53\%$ on \texttt{libero\_goal}. Additional analysis indicates that phase-level supervision and multi-frame directional cues make the student less sensitive to noisy teacher actions, such as erroneous high-frequency gripper changes. Overall, VLA-AD demonstrates that offline semantic guidance from VLMs can substantially improve the efficiency, robustness, and deployability of VLA policy distillation.

results.seo_title: Adaptive Multimodal Agents-Based Framework for Automatic Workflow…
results.meta_description: A novel framework for automatic workflow execution through a distinct two-phase pipeline leveraging Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation and graph-based…
results.body_html:
The proposed framework achieves automatic workflow execution through a distinct two-phase pipeline leveraging Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation and graph-based approach.

Offline discovery phase: architecture adaptsively constructs a topological knowledge base from fragmented execution logs. Inference phase: agents leverage Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation over the fixed, pre-established graph. Closed-loop collaborative verification protocol for dynamic self-correction and navigation.

Superior task decomposition and adaptive navigation performance. The graph-based approach facilitates superior task decomposition and adaptive navigation performance.

Dynamically self-correcting and navigating capabilities. The closed-loop collaborative verification protocol enables dynamic self-correction and navigation.

Validation High reliability and semantic awareness even with limited training data.

Conclusion The proposed framework demonstrates its ability to maintain high reliability and semantic awareness even with limited training data, making it a promising solution for automatic workflow execution.

source.title: Adaptive Multimodal Agents-Based Framework for Automatic Workflow Execution
source.summary: Researchers have developed an adaptive multimodal agents-based framework for automatic workflow execution that addresses the limitations of current methodologies. The proposed framework uses a two-phase pipeline to construct a topological knowledge base from execution logs during an offline phase, allowing agents to navigate complex workflows more effectively. This approach enables superior task decomposition and adaptive navigation performance, even with limited training data. The framework has been validated in a real-world context, demonstrating its reliability and semantic awareness capabilities.

results.seo_title: How AutoScientists Revolutionize Long-Running Scientific…
results.meta_description: Discover how AutoScientists improves biomedical machine learning, language-model training optimization, and protein fitness prediction with its decentralized…
results.body_html:
AutoScientists, a decentralized team of AI agents, improves long-running computational scientific experimentation by automating hypothesis generation, experiment design, and critique proposals.

Improving Biomedical Machine Learning

  • AutoScientists achieves a mean leaderboard percentile of 74.4% across 24 tasks on BioML-Bench, improving over the strongest AI agent by +8.33%
  • Biomedical imaging, protein engineering, single-cell omics, and drug discovery are among the tasks where AutoScientists excels

Boosting Language-Model Training Optimization

MethodValidation Bits-Per-Byte
AutoScientists1.9x faster than Autoresearch
GPT Training Optimization7 vs. 0 accepted improvements with single-agent approach

Advancing Protein Fitness Prediction

AutoScientists discovers a method for ACE2-Spike binding that improves over the current state-of-the-art model by +12.5% in Spearman correlation.

Read more about how AutoScientists is revolutionizing long-running scientific experimentation with its decentralized team of AI agents.

Read time: 10 minutes

source.title: AutoScientists: Self-Organizing Agent Teams for Long-Running Scientific Experimentation
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new approach to long-running scientific experimentation using AI agents that can self-organize into teams and share knowledge to improve results. The system, called AutoScientists, has been tested on various tasks in biomedical machine learning, language model training optimization, and protein fitness prediction, achieving significant improvements over existing approaches. In one test, AutoScientists improved the performance of a top-performing AI agent by 8.33% across multiple tasks.

results.seo_title: VeriTrip: A Verifiable Benchmark for Travel Planning Agents over…
results.meta_description: A new benchmark for travel planning agents that evaluates their robustness and reliability in handling unstructured web corpora.
results.body_html:
VeriTrip provides a rigorous foundation for the next generation of travel planning agents capable of operating in unconstrained, multimodal environments.

Establishes Multimodal Retrieval Base

Forces agents to autonomously orchestrate queries across heterogeneous data Derived from real-world sources Multimodal retrieval base (MRB) enables evidence-grounded reasoning

Synchronized Verifiable Knowledge Base

Verification ProtocolFactual Reliability

Cell-wise verification protocol Precise quantification of factual reliability

Distinguishes systematic reasoning failures from parametric hallucinations Enables rigorous evaluation of agent robustness and reliability

Critical Retrieval-Reasoning Trade-off Autonomous retrieval significantly erodes instruction retention, highlighting the need for a verifiable benchmark like VeriTrip.

Evaluations reveal critical retrieval-reasoning trade-off Cognitive load of autonomous retrieval significantly impacts instruction retention VeriTrip provides rigorous foundation for next generation of planning agents

source.title: VeriTrip: A Verifiable Benchmark for Travel Planning Agents over Unstructured Web Corpora
source.summary: Researchers have introduced VeriTrip, a new benchmark designed to evaluate travel planning agents' robustness and reliability in handling complex, unstructured web corpora. Unlike existing benchmarks, VeriTrip focuses on evidence-grounded reasoning and requires agents to autonomously orchestrate queries across heterogeneous data sources. This shift aims to better assess the cognitive load of autonomous retrieval and its impact on instruction retention. The benchmark provides a rigorous foundation for next-generation planning agents capable of operating in unconstrained environments.

results.seo_title: Agentic AI for Robot Teams: Scalable Architecture and Lessons Learned
results.meta_description: Discover the latest advancements in agentic AI for collaborative robotic teams, including a scalable architecture and practical lessons learned from ongoing…
results.body_html:
What is agentic AI for robot teams? Agentic AI refers to the ability of robots to make decisions and adapt to changing situations without human intervention. The Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory has made significant advancements in this area, developing a scalable architecture that enables autonomous behavior in multi-robot environments.

Enabling autonomy, coordination, and adaptability across heterogeneous systems Developing scalable architectures to support agentic behaviors Addressing challenges related to data management and communication between robots

The importance of human-robot collaboration in achieving successful outcomes The need for robust data management systems to support autonomous decision-making The challenges of integrating multiple AI agents and robots into a cohesive system

Future Work Directions As researchers continue to push the boundaries of agentic AI, we can expect significant advancements in areas such as autonomous navigation, object recognition, and decision-making.

source.title: Agentic AI for Robot Teams
source.summary:
This presentation highlights recent efforts at the Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory to advance agentic AI for collaborative robotic teams. It begins by framing the core challenges of enabling autonomy, coordination, and adaptability across heterogeneous systems, then introduces a scalable architecture designed to support agentic behaviors in multi-robot environments. The talk concludes with key challenges encountered and practical lessons learned from ongoing research and development.

Key learnings

Provides an introduction to LLM-based AI AgentsDescribes an approach to applying LLM-based AI Agents to robotic teamsProvides demonstrations of the approach running in hardware with a heterogeneous team of robotsPresents lessons learned and future work in this areaDownload this free whitepaper now!
